passages.explanation: The Festival of Unleavened Bread, called the Passover, is approaching. The chief priests and teachers of the law are looking for a way to get rid of Jesus because they fear the people. Then Satan enters Judas, called Iscariot, one of the Twelve. He goes to the chief priests and officers of the temple guard and discusses how he might betray Jesus. They're delighted and agree to give him money. He consents and watches for an opportunity to hand Jesus over when no crowd is present. Luke is the only Gospel that explicitly attributes Judas' betrayal to Satan's entry. The human conspiracy and the spiritual reality operate together — the authorities provide the motive, Judas provides the access, and Satan provides the push.

The day of Unleavened Bread arrives, when the Passover lamb must be sacrificed. Jesus sends Peter and John: go and make preparations for us to eat the Passover. They ask where. He tells them: as you enter the city, a man carrying a jar of water will meet you. Follow him to the house he enters and say to the owner: the Teacher asks, where is the guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples? He'll show you a large upper room, all furnished. Make preparations there. They go and find everything exactly as Jesus said. A man carrying water would have been unusual — that was typically women's work — making him easy to spot. Whether this is supernatural knowledge or prearranged signals, the effect is the same: Jesus is in control of the details.

When the hour comes, Jesus reclines at the table with the apostles. He says: I have eagerly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer. For I tell you, I will not eat it again until it finds fulfilment in the kingdom of God. He takes a cup, gives thanks, and says: take this and divide it among yourselves. I tell you, I will not drink again from the fruit of the vine until the kingdom of God comes. He takes bread, gives thanks, breaks it, and gives it to them: this is my body given for you — do this in remembrance of me. After the supper he takes the cup: this cup is the new covenant in my blood, which is poured out for you. But the hand of him who is going to betray me is with mine on the table. The Son of Man will go as it has been decreed. But woe to that man who betrays him! They begin to question among themselves which of them it might be. Jesus transforms the Passover meal into a new memorial — his body and blood replacing the lamb. The old covenant was sealed with animal blood at Sinai; the new covenant is sealed with his.

A dispute breaks out among the disciples about which of them is considered the greatest. Jesus says: the kings of the Gentiles lord it over them, and those who exercise authority call themselves benefactors. But you are not to be like that. The greatest among you should be like the youngest, and the one who rules like the one who serves. For who is greater — the one at the table or the one who serves? Isn't it the one at the table? But I am among you as one who serves. You are those who have stood by me in my trials. I confer on you a kingdom, just as my Father conferred one on me, so that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom and sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel. At the Last Supper, hours before the cross, the disciples are arguing about status. Jesus' patience is remarkable — he doesn't rebuke them but redefines greatness. And he promises them thrones, even knowing what's about to happen.

Simon, Simon — Satan has asked to sift all of you as wheat. But I have prayed for you, Simon, that your faith may not fail. And when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers. Peter replies: Lord, I'm ready to go with you to prison and to death. Jesus answers: I tell you, Peter, before the rooster crows today, you will deny three times that you know me. Jesus doesn't pray that Peter won't be tested — he prays that Peter's faith will survive the test. And he already sees past the failure to the restoration: 'when you have turned back.' The denial is certain. So is the recovery.

Jesus asks them: when I sent you without purse, bag, or sandals, did you lack anything? Nothing, they answer. He says: but now if you have a purse, take it, and also a bag. If you don't have a sword, sell your cloak and buy one. It is written — and he was numbered with the transgressors — and I tell you that this must be fulfilled in me. Yes, what is written about me is reaching its fulfilment. The disciples say: see, Lord, here are two swords. That's enough, he says. The shift in instructions signals a shift in circumstances. The earlier mission was under divine protection; now danger is real and the disciples will need to fend for themselves. But 'that's enough' to two swords suggests Jesus isn't planning an armed resistance — the swords serve a narrative purpose, fulfilling the 'numbered with transgressors' prophecy.

Jesus goes out as usual to the Mount of Olives, and the disciples follow. He says: pray that you won't fall into temptation. He withdraws about a stone's throw, kneels down, and prays: Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me — yet not my will, but yours be done. An angel from heaven appears and strengthens him. Being in anguish, he prays more earnestly, and his sweat becomes like drops of blood falling to the ground. When he rises from prayer and goes back to the disciples, he finds them asleep, exhausted from sorrow. He says: why are you sleeping? Get up and pray so that you won't fall into temptation. Luke alone records the angel and the bloody sweat. This is the Son of God in genuine agony — not performing suffering but experiencing it. He asks for another way and accepts there isn't one.

While Jesus is still speaking, a crowd appears. Judas, one of the Twelve, is leading them. He approaches Jesus to kiss him. Jesus says: Judas, are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss? When the followers see what's about to happen, they ask: Lord, should we strike with our swords? One of them strikes the high priest's servant, cutting off his right ear. Jesus says: no more of this! He touches the man's ear and heals it. Then he speaks to the chief priests, officers of the temple guard, and elders: am I leading a rebellion, that you come with swords and clubs? Every day I was with you in the temple courts, and you didn't lay a hand on me. But this is your hour — when darkness reigns. Jesus heals the ear of his captor. His last miracle before the cross is an act of compassion toward the people arresting him. 'This is your hour — when darkness reigns' acknowledges a temporary transfer of power. Evil gets its moment.

They seize Jesus, lead him away, and take him into the house of the high priest. Peter follows at a distance. A fire is kindled in the middle of the courtyard and Peter sits down with the others. A servant girl sees him by the firelight and stares: this man was with him. Peter denies it: woman, I don't know him. A little later someone else sees him: you also are one of them. Peter says: man, I am not. About an hour later another insists: certainly this fellow was with him — he's a Galilean. Peter replies: man, I don't know what you're talking about. Just as he's speaking, the rooster crows. The Lord turns and looks straight at Peter. Then Peter remembers what the Lord said: before the rooster crows today, you will disown me three times. He goes outside and weeps bitterly. Only Luke records the detail that Jesus turns and looks at Peter. It's the look, not the rooster, that breaks him.

The men guarding Jesus mock and beat him. They blindfold him and demand: prophesy! Who hit you? They heap many other insults on him. At daybreak the council of the elders, both chief priests and teachers of the law, meet together and Jesus is led before them. They say: if you are the Messiah, tell us. Jesus replies: if I tell you, you won't believe me, and if I asked you, you wouldn't answer. But from now on, the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the mighty God. They all ask: are you then the Son of God? He replies: you say that I am. They say: why do we need any more testimony? We've heard it from his own lips. Luke compresses the trial into a single morning session. Jesus' response is careful — he doesn't deny the titles but reframes them on his own terms. 'From now on' points to his exaltation, not his condemnation. They think they're ending his story; he knows it's beginning.
